text: Hi all, this seventy-year-old gentleman presented to our emergency department with upper abdominal pain of one week duration. On examination of the patient, tachycardia and tachypnea was there and there was severe tenderness in right hypochondrium and his total count was fourteen thousand. A CECT was taken and showed distended gallbladder with two large pericholecystic collections.

Our initial plan was a nematogated drainage of the collection and a cholecystostomy. And we discussed the same with our interventional radiologist. Since the collection is multiloculated and multiple in number, the approach was a bit difficult. So we decided to a laparoscopic drainage of the collections and a cholecystostomy if possible.

The initial laparoscopic view shows the left lobe of the liver and gallbladder covered with omentum, which is adhered to the anterior abdominal wall. The right iliac fossa, pelvis, left iliac fossa, and left paracolic gutter are visible, with some inflammatory fluid present. A second port is created in the right hypochondrium just below the right costal margin.

The surgeon introduces a suction apparatus to separate the omental adhesions, revealing pus that is collected in a syringe for culture and sensitivity study. The acute adhesions are easily separable with the suction apparatus.

The surgeon exposes the fundus of the gallbladder and attempts to separate the omentum from the gallbladder using blunt dissection. A new 5mm port is created in the epigastrium for access, with no energy source utilized during the dissection.
The surgeon performs a thorough peritoneal lavage using eight to ten liters of normal saline, visibly flushing the abscess cavity within the abdominal cavity.
Since the patient was hemodynamically unstable, our plan is do the minimum and come out. So we decided to proceed a cholecystostomy and stop the procedure.

Following thorough peritoneal lavage, the surgeon performs a cholecystostomy by introducing an SPC trocar to pierce the gallbladder. The trocar is then removed, and a 14 French Foley's tube is inserted into the gallbladder.
The surgeon performs another wash of the surgical area and inserts a 32 French drainage tube, positioning it in the sub-hepatic space.
